risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "ASUs will be stratified and randomised... Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process using random number generating software, with stratification and an independent statistician generating the sequence, which supports truly random sequence generation.

Quote: "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software... the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician." Comment: The protocol explicitly states that the allocation sequence was concealed until assignment and that an independent offshore statistician performed the randomisation, which indicates recruiters could not foresee upcoming cluster allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Both the medical director and NUM of all consenting ASUs will be aware that our study is examining the effect of an intervention..." and "control ASUs may be able to deduce their group allocation because no workshops are being organised." Also, "Patients will be blinded to group allocation." Comment: This was a cluster service-delivery intervention, so the personnel delivering care were not fully blinded and control units could infer allocation; because the outcomes include clinician behaviour and patient outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, the dominant risk for this domain is high despite patient blinding.

Quote: "All CATIs will be undertaken by research interviewers blind to the study design and also to ASU group allocation" and "Data entry will be undertaken by the CATI research assistants blind to group allocation." Comment: The main patient-reported outcomes (e.g., mRS, Barthel Index, SF-36) were collected by blinded telephone interviewers, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement. Although some professional behaviour outcomes were obtained by independent research assistants blind to allocation, the key patient outcome assessment relevant here was blinded.

Summary: The provided text is a study protocol and does not report actual follow-up completion or missing outcome data for the analysed results. It states the planned approach: "Intention-to-treat analysis will applied" and that follow-up would occur by 90-day CATI, with a sample size calculation assuming "loss to follow-up of 10%", but there are no observed per-arm attrition numbers, exclusions, or results indicating whether missing data were balanced or substantial. Comment: Because no realised attrition data are reported here, the risk of bias from incomplete outcome data cannot be judged from the available text.
